mnemonics.title: CLIMB the Hill
mnemonics.content: C = K*P mod 26 (Cipher), Linear algebra, Invertible matrix, Modular arithmetic, Block encryption. CLIMB reminds you of the Hill Cipher's core concepts.

mnemonics.title: KEY-DIA Rule
mnemonics.content: Key must have Determinant Inverse Available: gcd(det(K), 26) = 1. If the determinant shares a factor with 26, the key is invalid.

mnemonics.title: DAD Decrypts
mnemonics.content: Determinant inverse, Adjugate matrix, then Dot product (multiply). K^-1 = d^-1 * adj(K) mod 26 -- three steps to find the inverse key.

mnemonics.title: KPA Kills Hill
mnemonics.content: Known-Plaintext Attack kills Hill cipher. If attacker knows m plaintext-ciphertext pairs, they solve K = C * P^-1 mod 26. Linear = vulnerable.

mnemonics.title: 26's Bad Factors
mnemonics.content: 26 = 2 * 13. So determinant values that are multiples of 2 or 13 (like 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 13, 14...) make invalid keys. Only odd, non-13-multiples work.

keyPoints: Hill Cipher is a polygraphic substitution cipher based on linear algebra.
Encryption requires a square, invertible key matrix modulo 26.
Decryption requires finding the modular inverse of the key matrix determinant and its adjugate.
Vulnerable to known-plaintext attacks due to its linear nature.
Block size m determines how many letters are encrypted simultaneously.
commonMistakes: Using a key matrix that is not invertible modulo 26 (i.e., its determinant and 26 are not coprime).
Forgetting to apply the modulo 26 operation to each element of the resulting vector during both encryption and decryption.
Confusing the adjugate matrix with the transpose matrix.
Forgetting to pad plaintext when its length is not a multiple of m.
